vocabularies.name: currentStatus
vocabularies.description: Development status of a tool, service, or reference resource.

terms.description: Widely adopted, stable, and actively maintained.
In production use within at least some communities, with reasonable stability.
Developing, with active work and growing adoption.
Working examples exist but not yet ready for general use.
Idea articulated and scoped, but no working examples yet.
Identified as needed but not yet started.

vocabularies.name: scopeValue
vocabularies.description: Organisational or geographic scope at which something operates.

terms.description: Operates at the level of a single person.
Operates within the bounds of a specific project.
Operates within a working team, potentially spanning projects.
Operates at the level of a single institution.
Operates across multiple institutions.
Operates across a sector or domain (e.g. museums, heritage science).
Operates at national level.
Operates across multiple countries.
Operates globally with no national or regional restriction.

vocabularies.name: categoryValue
vocabularies.description: Category of a tool or service entry.

terms.description: A documented body of knowledge that others build on, such as a schema, vocabulary, or template set.
Software tools, libraries, or applications that perform specific tasks.
Long-running services or systems that others depend on.
Procedures, training materials, and best practice guidance.
Integrated systems combining several tools and services.
Ongoing operational service provided to users.
Other categories not covered above.

vocabularies.name: commonlyPresent
vocabularies.description: How frequently a resource type is present in typical heritage science work.

terms.description: Present in nearly all heritage science work.
Present in most heritage science work.
Present in some heritage science work, depending on project type and resources.
Present in a minority of heritage science work.
Present only in specific kinds of heritage science work.

vocabularies.name: fairPrinciple
vocabularies.description: The four FAIR principles. Within this framework, a level may achieve a principle locally (within the team, project, or institution) or externally (across institutional boundaries through the data itself). The framework treats both as legitimate achievements while being honest about the distinction.

terms.description: Data should be findable through clear identifiers and indexed metadata. Local findability arrives when a documented schema and consistent records make resources retrievable within the team or institution. External findability requires publication or registration that makes the resource discoverable by those outside the institution, which typically needs persistent identifiers and aggregator participation.

Data should be retrievable through standardised protocols, with clear access conditions. Local accessibility arrives when the resource is documented and stored well enough that team members can retrieve it. External accessibility requires infrastructure that exposes the resource to retrieval by external parties under documented conditions.

Data should use shared standards and vocabularies so it can be combined with other data. Local interoperability begins with a documented schema that ensures equivalent fields appear in equivalent places. External interoperability requires that the schema and vocabularies are shared with other organisations, which begins at T04a and strengthens through T04b and T05.

Data should be well described and carry clear re-use licences so it can be used in new contexts. Local reusability arrives with documented schema and clear local conventions. External reusability additionally requires explicit re-use licences and community-recognised standards, both of which typically become expected at T04b.

vocabularies.name: fairScope
vocabularies.description: Whether a FAIR achievement is local (within the team, project, or institution) or external (across institutional boundaries through the data itself). Introduced by this framework to recognise that significant FAIR work happens locally before external FAIR is achievable. Note that external FAIR can also be supported by infrastructures beyond the data itself, such as access frameworks or aggregators, but that supported external FAIR is outside the scope of this framework as documented here.

terms.description: Achievement is internal to the team, project, or institution. Local FAIR contributions are real and worthwhile, and represent the practical first achievements of researchers and teams developing their data practice.

Achievement is across institutional boundaries, delivered by the data and its metadata themselves rather than by human or infrastructural intermediaries. This is the strict reading of the published FAIR principles.

vocabularies.name: transitionKind
vocabularies.description: The kind of transition between levels or across the framework.

terms.description: A transition from one specific treatment level to another.
Movement from a higher level to a lower level, typically for exchange with partners working at the lower level.
A transition that applies across multiple levels rather than between two specific levels.
